claims.claim_text: The opening December 2023 bare-metal Gatekeeper outage anecdote names a real firm, date, scale, duration, and business impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In December 2023, a multinational financial services firm running a 600-node bare-metal Kubernetes platform suffered a six-hour outage that began with a routine policy push. A developer added a single OPA Gatekeeper constraint requiring every pod to declare resource limits and the team rolled it out cluster-wide on a Friday afternoon. Within minutes, the cluster began rejecting CoreDNS pod restarts, Cilium agent updates, and even the Gatekeeper webhook's own self-managed deployment. Service-to-service DNS lookups began failing as old CoreDNS pods rotated off cordoned nodes, and the control plane self-quarantined as health probes timed out. By the time the on-call engineer realised the problem, half the cluster's payment authorisation services were returning 503s, and the postmortem would later trace the cascade to a single missing namespace exemption in the constraint manifest.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A cluster-wide admission policy change can cascade into control-plane instability if critical system namespaces or dependencies are not exempted. For example, a policy that blocks essential system pods from restarting can turn a routine rollout into a widespread outage.
claims.rationale: This is a named incident with precise operational details but no attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: Pod Security Admission replaced PodSecurityPolicy; PSP was deprecated in v1.21 and removed in v1.25, while PSA reached stable in v1.25 and defines the privileged, baseline, and restricted levels with namespace label modes such as enforce, audit, and warn.
claims.span_hint: section: The Governance Architecture, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: The shared PSA source directly covers feature state, label-based configuration, and the three policy levels.

claims.claim_text: Mutating admission webhooks run before validating admission webhooks, and validating webhooks see the post-mutation object after API-server validation.
claims.span_hint: section: The Governance Architecture, diagram explanation and following paragraph
claims.rationale: Kubernetes dynamic admission docs explicitly describe mutating webhooks first and validating webhooks after object validation.

claims.claim_text: The webhook-deadlock caution box includes an uncited retailer outage anecdote and other precise historical specifics.
claims.span_hint: section: The Governance Architecture, caution box
claims.anchor_text: A platform team deployed a validating webhook with `failurePolicy: Fail` covering all namespaces, including `kube-system`. When the nodes hosting the policy engine crashed, the policy engine pods could not be rescheduled because the API server could not reach the webhook to validate the new pods. The cluster deadlocked. The same class of failure took down a Kubernetes 1.23 cluster at a major retailer in 2022 during a routine kernel patch rollout, where a CNI restart simultaneously evicted the only surviving Kyverno replica. Always use `failurePolicy: Ignore` for the namespace hosting your policy engine, or explicitly exempt it using a `namespaceSelector` or a `MatchConditions` expression. This is not a "best practice" in the soft sense; it is the difference between a cluster that recovers itself and one that has to be reset by hand.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A validating webhook that covers critical system namespaces can deadlock a cluster if the webhook service becomes unavailable, because replacement pods may be blocked from admission. Exempt critical namespaces such as `kube-system` and the policy engine's own namespace, and scope requests carefully with `namespaceSelector` or `matchConditions` so the cluster can recover without manual intervention.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is valid, but the named incident and exact version/date details are not sourced.

claims.claim_text: Admission webhook match conditions were added in v1.27 and moved to beta, enabled by default, in Kubernetes v1.28.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy Libraries and Exemption Workflows, paragraph on `MatchConditions`
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes v1.28 release notes specifically describe the feature timeline for admission webhook match conditions.

claims.claim_text: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy is stable starting in Kubernetes v1.30 and provides in-process CEL-based validation as an alternative to validating admission webhooks.
claims.span_hint: section: Native Validating Admission Policy with CEL, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The shared VAP source directly states the feature state and in-process CEL-based model.

claims.claim_text: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy supports `validationActions` of `Deny`, `Warn`, and `Audit`, and `Deny` and `Warn` cannot be used together.
claims.span_hint: section: Native Validating Admission Policy with CEL, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The shared VAP source explicitly lists the supported validation actions and the Deny/Warn restriction.

claims.claim_text: MutatingAdmissionPolicy is beta in Kubernetes v1.34 and requires the `MutatingAdmissionPolicy` feature gate plus `--runtime-config=admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1beta1=true` on kube-apiserver.
claims.span_hint: section: Native Validating Admission Policy with CEL, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The MutatingAdmissionPolicy docs state the beta feature state and required control-plane flags.

claims.claim_text: If any configured admission controller rejects a request, the request is rejected; for validating webhooks, if any matching webhook rejects, the request fails.
claims.span_hint: section: Native Validating Admission Policy with CEL, stop-and-think answer paragraph
claims.rationale: Kubernetes admission control docs describe both the two-phase process and the rule that any rejection fails the request.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper adds Kubernetes-native constraint CRDs, constraint templates, mutation support, audit functionality, and external data support on top of OPA.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The Gatekeeper repository README lists these Kubernetes-specific capabilities explicitly.

claims.claim_text: Open Policy Agent became a CNCF graduated project in 2021.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page gives the graduation date for OPA.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper mutation was promoted to stable in v3.10.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper, paragraph on mutation
claims.rationale: The v3.10.0 Gatekeeper release notes explicitly say mutation is promoted to stable.

claims.claim_text: CEL-based policies enforced through Gatekeeper became GA in v3.18.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper, paragraph on mutation/CEL
claims.rationale: The v3.18.0 Gatekeeper release notes explicitly mark CEL-based policies as GA.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper VAP integration is beta in v3.20 and enabled by default for the native CEL path.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper, paragraph on mutation/CEL
claims.rationale: The v3.20.0 Gatekeeper release notes describe VAP integration as beta and enabled by default.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper separates reusable policy definition from policy instantiation via `ConstraintTemplate` and `Constraint` resources.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper, paragraph on architecture
claims.rationale: This is a real core Gatekeeper concept, but the best primary explanation is on Gatekeeper's off-allowlist documentation site.

claims.claim_text: Kyverno moved to CNCF Graduated maturity on March 16, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Kyverno, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page records the exact graduation date.

claims.claim_text: Kyverno can validate, mutate, generate, and clean up resources using Kubernetes admission controls and background scans.
claims.span_hint: section: Kyverno, opening and differentiators paragraphs
claims.rationale: The Kyverno repository README lists these capabilities directly.

claims.claim_text: The stated deprecation/removal timeline for legacy `ClusterPolicy` and `CleanupPolicy` kinds is too specific and not backed by the upstream evidence found.
claims.span_hint: section: Kyverno, paragraph on CEL transition
claims.anchor_text: Consequently, the older `ClusterPolicy` and `CleanupPolicy` types were deprecated in v1.17, entered a critical-fixes-only phase in v1.18 and v1.19, and are planned for complete removal in v1.20.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kyverno is steering new policy authoring toward its CEL-based policy APIs, so teams standardising on Kyverno should check the current release notes and migration guidance before committing to older policy kinds.
claims.rationale: Available upstream evidence supports CEL API evolution, but not this exact legacy-kind deprecation/removal timeline.

claims.claim_text: Kyverno v1.14 added CEL-based `ValidatingPolicy` support and auto-generated `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` from that policy type.
claims.span_hint: section: Kyverno, paragraph on CEL transition
claims.rationale: The v1.14.0 release notes explicitly mention `ValidatingPolicy` support and generated VAP resources.

claims.claim_text: Kyverno's new CEL policy APIs moved through a staged alpha/beta/v1 transition around the v1.17 release window.
claims.span_hint: section: Kyverno, paragraph on CEL transition
claims.rationale: The tracked migration issue includes an explicit version matrix for the new `policies.kyverno.io` APIs.

claims.claim_text: The precise `10,000+` threshold for background-scan resource spikes is unsupported.
claims.span_hint: section: Comparison: Gatekeeper vs Kyverno, note box
claims.anchor_text: In clusters with 10,000+ ConfigMaps or Secrets, these scans can cause severe CPU and memory spikes that masquerade as a noisy neighbour problem.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In large clusters, background scans can create noticeable CPU and memory spikes, so they should be tuned and capacity-tested before rollout.
claims.rationale: The operational risk is plausible, but the exact cluster-size threshold is not sourced.

claims.claim_text: The claim that both engines maintain exhaustive libraries covering Pod Security Standards, CIS, and many community rules is broader than the evidence found.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy Libraries and Exemption Workflows, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Both projects maintain exhaustive libraries that cover the Pod Security Standards (Restricted and Baseline), the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark, and dozens of opinionated best-practice rules contributed by the community.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Both projects maintain large policy libraries covering common Kubernetes security and governance patterns, so starting from those libraries is usually faster and safer than writing every policy from scratch.
claims.rationale: The existence of policy libraries is easy to verify, but the exact coverage claim is too sweeping as written.

claims.claim_text: `gator test` is presented as a local Gatekeeper policy test workflow for CI.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy CI/CD and Testing, Gatekeeper paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a real Gatekeeper CLI capability, but the primary reference is on Gatekeeper's off-allowlist docs site.

claims.claim_text: `kyverno test` is presented as a YAML-driven local test workflow for Kyverno policies.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy CI/CD and Testing, Kyverno paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a real Kyverno CLI capability, but the authoritative user documentation is on Kyverno's off-allowlist docs site.

claims.claim_text: Falco is a CNCF graduated project.
claims.span_hint: section: Runtime Security: Falco vs Tetragon, Falco subsection opening
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page records Falco's graduation status and date.

claims.claim_text: Falco's syscall capture path uses either a kernel module or an eBPF probe, with userspace libraries reading events from a ring buffer.
claims.span_hint: section: Runtime Security: Falco vs Tetragon, Falco architecture paragraphs
claims.rationale: The Falco libs repository describes the kernel module/eBPF drivers and the userspace ring-buffer path.

claims.claim_text: Tetragon is an eBPF-based runtime enforcement and observability tool that can react to security-significant events.
claims.span_hint: section: Runtime Security: Falco vs Tetragon, Tetragon subsection opening
claims.rationale: The Tetragon repository README explicitly describes real-time eBPF-based runtime enforcement and reaction to security-significant events.

claims.claim_text: The exact Tetragon kernel floor and preference statement (`5.10` hard floor, `6.x` preferred) is too specific for the evidence gathered.
claims.span_hint: section: Runtime Security: Falco vs Tetragon, Tetragon subsection
claims.anchor_text: Tetragon requires modern kernels with BTF (BPF Type Format) enabled, currently 5.10 or later as a hard floor and 6.x preferred.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Tetragon depends on modern kernels with the required eBPF and BTF capabilities enabled, so kernel compatibility should be validated early in any rollout.
claims.rationale: Kernel/BTF dependence is real, but the exact floor/preference values were not confirmed from an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The `100ms` p99 webhook-latency threshold is an unsupported prescriptive benchmark.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy CI/CD and Testing, final paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Any policy whose evaluation pushes the p99 webhook latency above 100ms is a production risk and should be optimised or split before rollout.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Any policy that adds material webhook latency under representative load is a production risk and should be optimised or split before rollout.
claims.rationale: The performance-testing recommendation is sensible, but the exact numeric threshold is not sourced.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Validating Admission Policy
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for native CEL-based admission validation, bindings, match conditions, and validation actions.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Admission Webhook Good Practices
further_reading.why_relevant: Best source for webhook scoping, failure behavior, latency, and deadlock-avoidance guidance.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Pod Security Admission
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative source for PSA labels, levels, and the built-in pod-hardening model that replaced PSP-era workflows.

further_reading.title: Falco Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the current project scope, runtime-detection model, and adjacent documentation links for Falco deployments.

further_reading.title: Tetragon Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the current project scope, runtime-enforcement model, and adjacent documentation links for Tetragon policies.

notes: Focused on prose claims outside the Quiz and Hands-On Exercise per task rules. Code blocks were not seeded. Table-only facts were only captured when they introduced unique version/capability claims already echoed in prose. Several Kyverno and Gatekeeper CLI/details claims are real but need allowlist expansion because their best primary docs live on off-allowlist project documentation sites.
